steps.title: Identify what the data show
steps.content: The experiment gave sunflower sea stars a choice between a nutritionally rich urchin and a nutritionally poor urchin (the kind found in barrens). The sea stars selected the rich urchin 42.7% of the time and the poor urchin 37.5% of the time. The rates are close, meaning the sea stars did not consistently avoid the nutritionally poor urchins even when a better option was available. Any valid conclusion must be grounded in this result.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'sunflower sea stars are willing to hunt sea urchins, but if given a choice, they will prey on other more nutritious marine animals instead.': The study tested only sea urchins; no other marine animals were offered or measured. This choice invents information not in the text. ✗
  • Choice B — 'sunflower sea stars are reluctant to feed on both nutritionally poor and nutritionally rich sea urchins and are therefore unlikely to thrive in kelp forests.': A 37.5% selection rate for poor urchins is not reluctance — the sea stars actively ate them. This misreads the data. ✗
  • Choice C — 'sunflower sea stars are less likely to consume sea urchins in barrens than other species of sea stars are, putting sunflower sea stars at a high risk of extinction.': No other sea star species were tested; the study makes no such comparison. ✗
  • Choice D — 'sunflower sea stars do not always avoid foraging on nutritionally poor sea urchins, making sunflower sea star population recovery a potentially important tool for controlling urchin barrens.': Directly reflects the 37.5% selection rate for poor urchins and logically extends it: if sea stars eat the poor urchins found in barrens, recovering their populations could help clear those barrens. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
